item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the original source has been deleted and no source found indicates it's still available under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone could be better, and the object itself is not really interesting. So I'll downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These expungements aren't good, the picture is off-putting, and it's only tangentially weird; a person inhabiting an object that talks and gets distressed is okay but not enough to save it. The radiation factor seems silly as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to be ok with this, but the expungements and redactions just make me cringe, so -1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't shake the feeling that it's a 3D model from Half-Life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was waffling as to whether "x-ray machine possessed by ghost" was really worth upvoting, then I got to the addendum. That said, this is highly reminiscent of one that went up recently, the guy who's bilocated in a coma and in his normal life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Passive scans reveal no deviations between the internal structure of SCP-1966 and other body scanners using the same design.

The voice emanates from resonant vibrations in the [REDACTED], and no cause for these vibrations has yet been determined.

If there doesn't seem to be anything unusualy about its internal structure then there's no reason to redact the part of the device the voice is coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, it would make sense for the radiation levels to increase when it becomes stressed. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I like this a lot more now that I haven't just seen it. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a lot of SCPs that fit the "ordinary person's consciousness is trapped/copied in an object" thing. The first one that came to mind for me was SCP-086, but there are several others. That's not to say that this premise can't be used again, but it does mean that anything else using this premise is going to need something more to differentiate it from the others and make it interesting. But the only special property this has is the radiation, which felt like a tacked-on "oh by the way it's also deadly" to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This ^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the radiation. It makes me feel like whatever imprinting process have occured have side effects and consequences, and that it isn't just random magic that works but rather something paranormal that works in unnatural ways. It also adds to an already pretty horrific situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of the expungements rustle my jimmies. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't see the original, but I like this as it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for the repeated posting. Previous posts were stalling in the posting process and didn't look like they'd made it through. Mods feel free to delete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for the repeated posting. Previous posts were stalling in the posting process and didn't look like they'd made it through. Mods feel free to delete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keeping the massive radiation and the containment procedures that necessitates after changing it over from a nuclear bomb seems like a gratuitous holdover.

It's a machine traditionally used to scan people and you've eliminated any possibility of intersting anomalous behavior related to that function. I suggest greatly reducing the danger and containment, and upping the weirdness. Withholding vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you might want to change the picture, it looks computer generated, Im sure you can find a good picture on the internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know the radiation was a leftover of when this was a nuke, but you really need to mention earlier that it's radioactive. As it is right now, unless I'm missing something the earliest mention of radiation is 4 paragraphs into the description, when it says that its radiation output spikes when it's agitated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the creepiest thing about this, and the one with the most potential to the foundation, is the imprinting process.

The radiation feels like it tacked on, but the imprinting would make it a fine SCP - even as a Safe or Euclid class object.

What part of SCP-1966 houses the personality? Has anyone tried to find out? What happens if someone tries to operate the scanner?
This is the Foundation, you know they've tried to scan people in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything else that's been said above, plus:

  • the unnecessary capitalization of 'psychologist'
  • a reference to an Addendum 1 that isn't designated as such
  • It is [MEASUREMENTS] sounds off
  • 'sentient consciousness' is a tautology

And unfortunately, it is still very similar to SCP-1286, the atomic bomb is not what makes them similar.

item.title: I like it.
item.content:encoded:
I've only seen the revision from 8 minutes ago, but I like it. I have to agree with sanrewswann, a bomb doesn't work.

I like it. (In case that was buried).

The containment method seems a bit out-of-character for the Foundation, but someone might be being more cautious than usual.

Where the "exploratory surgery" log? e.g., "When the year panel was removed [REDACTED]" or "Since we have no effective anesthetic for SCP-1966, there will be no experimental disassembly, partial or full. The levels of radiation released were enough to interrupt the connection with our probe, even using a shielded wired connection."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bunker 10km underground for a talking airport x-ray scanner? A bit excessive, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would definitely change the containment procedures. The deepest borehole ever drilled was only 12km deep, and took a huge amount of time and effort to dig. And the temperature down there would be in excess of 100 degrees Centigrade, so a massive amount of cooling would be required too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I toned down the containment procedures. They were left over from when this was a 9 megaton warhead. And it isn't 100km deep, just 100km away from population and other SCPs. (I suspect in Nevada somewhere around an old nuke testing site.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Revamp of a SCP I almost posted last night. It was originally a thermonuclear weapon, then I was directed to SCP-1286, and it was hard to deny the articles were way too similar, even though the only real similarity was the idea of a talking nuke…

Removing the bomb not only removed 90% of the overlap with SCP-1286, but helped ratchet up the weirdness factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay…honestly the only big problems with the x-ray scanner that thinks its a person are 1) your data expungeds should just be spelled out, since right now they aren't interesting, and 2) Nothing in the object indicates why the heck it's 100km below ground in a room with 27 meter thick concrete walls. That would be….so expensive.
